stem: A 41-year-old woman attends your general practice with a 2-year history of heavy menstrual bleeding causing her to miss work for 1 to 2 days each month. Periods are predictable every 28 days but soak through pads hourly for the first 2 days. She has 2 children and her family is complete. There is no intermenstrual or postcoital bleeding. Pelvic examination is normal. Cervical screening is up to date and unremarkable. Transvaginal ultrasound shows a uniform endometrium 6 mm thick with no fibroids or polyps. She would like a long-term solution and is open to hormonal therapy.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 124/76 mmHg, HR 78 bpm, BMI 27 kg/m2
data_table.Examination: Pelvic exam normal, no adnexal masses
data_table.Investigations: Hb 108 g/L, ferritin 12 micrograms/L, thyroid function normal, beta-hCG negative, TVUS endometrium 6 mm uniform

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line management?

options.text: Referral for total laparoscopic hysterectomy as definitive treatment given her completed family
options.rationale: Hysterectomy is definitive but reserved for women in whom medical and minimally invasive options have failed or are unacceptable. Surgical morbidity and recovery time mean it is not first-line. (Source: RANZCOG - Heavy Menstrual Bleeding)

options.text: Combined oral contraceptive pill 30 micrograms ethinyloestradiol with norethisterone continuously
options.rationale: The combined oral contraceptive reduces menstrual blood loss by approximately 40 to 50%, less than the levonorgestrel IUS, and is contraindicated in women over 35 with additional cardiovascular risk factors. It is a reasonable second choice when an IUS is declined. (Source: RANZCOG - Heavy Menstrual Bleeding)

options.text: Tranexamic acid 1 g orally three times daily on heavy days only as a non-hormonal option
options.rationale: Tranexamic acid reduces blood loss by approximately 40 to 50% and is appropriate for women who decline hormonal therapy or seek symptom relief on heavy days only. It is less effective than a levonorgestrel IUS and does not provide contraception. (Source: RANZCOG - Heavy Menstrual Bleeding)

options.text: Referral for endometrial ablation as definitive treatment with preservation of the uterus
options.rationale: Endometrial ablation is appropriate when medical options have failed, are contraindicated, or the woman has firmly declined long-acting reversible contraception. It is not first-line when medical options have not yet been trialled. (Source: RANZCOG - Heavy Menstrual Bleeding)

options.text: Insertion of a 52 mg levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine system with oral iron supplementation
options.rationale: For women with heavy menstrual bleeding without structural cause who do not wish to conceive, the 52 mg levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine system is first-line in Australia. It reduces menstrual blood loss by approximately 90% at 6 months and treats coexistent iron deficiency anaemia by reducing ongoing losses. (Source: RANZCOG - Heavy Menstrual Bleeding)

explanation.summary: The Australian first-line approach to heavy menstrual bleeding without structural pathology is the 52 mg levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine system, which reduces blood loss by approximately 90% and provides contraception. Tranexamic acid and combined oral contraceptive are reasonable medical alternatives if an IUS is declined. Endometrial ablation and hysterectomy are reserved for failed or declined medical management. Concomitant iron deficiency is treated alongside the underlying bleeding control.
key_points: 52 mg levonorgestrel-releasing IUS reduces menstrual loss by approximately 90%
Tranexamic acid and COCP reduce loss by approximately 40 to 50%
Endometrial ablation and hysterectomy are second-line after failed or declined medical management
Iron deficiency anaemia is treated alongside the underlying cause
explanation.pearls: The levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine system is licensed for 8 years for contraception in Australia and 5 years for heavy menstrual bleeding indication, with maximal blood-loss reduction reached around 6 months after insertion.

stem: You are the obstetric registrar called urgently to the birth suite to review a 34-year-old primigravida at 33+2 weeks gestation with persistent severe hypertension despite oral labetalol 200 mg given 30 minutes ago. She reports a severe frontal headache and one episode of vomiting. Reflexes are brisk with 3 beats of clonus. Urine dipstick shows 4+ protein. Fetal CTG is reassuring. Intravenous access is established. The consultant is en route. The midwife has prepared the typical antihypertensive options for severe pre-eclampsia.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 178/116 mmHg (confirmed on 2 readings), HR 92 bpm, RR 18, SpO2 99% room air
data_table.Examination: Brisk reflexes, 3 beats clonus, no focal deficit, fundoscopy normal, mild facial oedema
data_table.Investigations: Platelets 142 x10^9/L, ALT 64 U/L, creatinine 78 micromol/L, uric acid 0.48 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate antihypertensive intervention?

options.text: Hydralazine 5 mg intravenously over 2 minutes, repeat after 20 minutes if target not reached
options.rationale: Severe hypertension persisting after oral labetalol requires intravenous treatment. Hydralazine 5 mg IV bolus repeated at 20 minutes is the SOMANZ first-line intravenous antihypertensive for severe hypertension in pregnancy where oral therapy has been inadequate. The target is systolic 140 to 160 and diastolic 90 to 100, not normotension. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy Guidelines)

options.text: Sublingual nifedipine 10 mg capsule punctured for rapid release with 5-minute reassessment
options.rationale: Sublingual short-acting nifedipine causes rapid uncontrolled hypotension and is not recommended in pregnancy. Immediate-release oral nifedipine 10 mg is acceptable but never sublingual. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy Guidelines)

options.text: Sodium nitroprusside infusion titrated to systolic blood pressure under 130 mmHg
options.rationale: Nitroprusside accumulates cyanide and thiocyanate metabolites in the foetus and is contraindicated in pregnancy except in life-threatening hypertensive emergency where no alternative is available. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy Guidelines)

options.text: Captopril 25 mg orally to address renin-angiotensin contribution to placental hypertension
options.rationale: ACE inhibitors are absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy because of fetal renal injury and oligohydramnios. They have no role in acute antenatal blood pressure control. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy Guidelines)

options.text: Furosemide 40 mg intravenously to reduce intravascular volume and lower blood pressure
options.rationale: Pre-eclampsia is characterised by intravascular volume contraction despite oedema. Loop diuretics worsen placental perfusion and are reserved for pulmonary oedema, not antihypertensive therapy. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Severe hypertension in pregnancy (systolic 160 or more, or diastolic 110 or more) requires urgent control to reduce maternal stroke risk while maintaining placental perfusion. The Australian first-line options are oral labetalol, oral immediate-release nifedipine, or intravenous hydralazine. Targets are systolic 140 to 160 and diastolic 90 to 100, not full normotension. Magnesium sulfate is also administered for severe pre-eclampsia regardless of seizure occurrence to reduce eclampsia risk by approximately 50%.
key_points: First-line antihypertensives in severe pre-eclampsia: labetalol, oral nifedipine immediate release, IV hydralazine
Targets: systolic 140 to 160, diastolic 90 to 100, not normotension
Magnesium sulfate 4 g loading then 1 g/h for seizure prophylaxis in severe pre-eclampsia
ACE inhibitors, ARBs, nitroprusside, and sublingual nifedipine are contraindicated in pregnancy
explanation.pearls: In severe pre-eclampsia, magnesium sulfate is given for seizure prophylaxis regardless of whether seizures have occurred, with a 4 g loading dose over 15 to 20 minutes then 1 g/hour for 24 hours after birth or last seizure.

sources.label: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular - Hypertension in Pregnancy

stem: You are the obstetric registrar at a regional hospital. A multiparous woman at 39+6 weeks is delivering. The head has been born but turtle sign is observed and routine downward traction has failed to deliver the anterior shoulder. The midwife has called for help. The clock has been started. The maternal head-of-bed has been flattened and her position has been adjusted with thighs flexed onto her abdomen. Suprapubic pressure has been applied for 30 seconds without success. The estimated time since head delivery is 1 minute 20 seconds. No episiotomy was performed.

data_table.Examination: Head delivered, restitution complete, anterior shoulder impacted on symphysis, no cord prolapse
data_table.Investigations: Estimated fetal weight at last antenatal review 4150 g

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next manoeuvre?

options.text: Cease attempts and prepare for an immediate caesarean section in the operating theatre
options.rationale: Caesarean is not feasible once the head has delivered and the trunk is impacted. The Zavanelli manoeuvre (replacement of the head) is last resort, performed only after all other manoeuvres fail. (Source: RANZCOG - Shoulder Dystocia Statement)

options.text: Apply fundal pressure to push the anterior shoulder past the symphysis during traction
options.rationale: Fundal pressure is contraindicated in shoulder dystocia because it worsens impaction by driving the shoulder further onto the symphysis and increases uterine rupture and brachial plexus injury rates. (Source: RANZCOG - Shoulder Dystocia Statement)

options.text: Perform symphysiotomy under local anaesthesia to widen the pelvic outlet immediately
options.rationale: Symphysiotomy is a last-resort procedure carrying significant maternal morbidity and is not appropriate before internal manoeuvres and all-fours position have been attempted. (Source: RANZCOG - Shoulder Dystocia Statement)

options.text: Continue applying downward traction with greater force while the McRoberts position is maintained
options.rationale: Increased traction force is the principal modifiable risk factor for neonatal brachial plexus injury. Routine axial traction is acceptable, but increased downward traction is harmful. (Source: RANZCOG - Shoulder Dystocia Statement)

options.text: Internal rotational manoeuvre to deliver the posterior shoulder by reaching in and rotating 180 degrees
options.rationale: After McRoberts and suprapubic pressure have failed, the next step is an internal manoeuvre: either delivery of the posterior arm or internal rotation. Both are considered first-line second-tier in the RANZCOG shoulder dystocia algorithm with comparable success rates. The HELPERR mnemonic places internal manoeuvres before all-fours and final-resort measures. (Source: RANZCOG - Shoulder Dystocia Statement)

explanation.summary: Shoulder dystocia is an obstetric emergency requiring a structured manoeuvre sequence. HELPERR captures the order: call for Help, evaluate Episiotomy need, Legs (McRoberts), suprapubic Pressure, Enter (internal manoeuvres), Remove the posterior arm, and Roll the patient (all fours). Internal rotational manoeuvres include Rubin II, Wood's screw, and reverse Wood's screw. Brachial plexus injury, hypoxic-ischaemic encephalopathy, and humeral fracture are recognised neonatal complications, while postpartum haemorrhage and perineal trauma affect the mother.
key_points: Fundal pressure is contraindicated; suprapubic pressure is the correct external manoeuvre
Internal manoeuvres follow McRoberts plus suprapubic pressure when those fail
Excess downward traction is the main modifiable cause of brachial plexus injury
Zavanelli replacement of the head is the last-resort manoeuvre
explanation.pearls: In shoulder dystocia, the next manoeuvre after McRoberts and suprapubic pressure is internal rotation or posterior arm delivery, not stronger traction - excess axial traction is the main modifiable risk for permanent brachial plexus injury.

stem: You are the obstetric registrar counselling a 34-year-old woman at 30 weeks in her second pregnancy. Her first birth 3 years ago was a lower-segment caesarean section for breech presentation at 39 weeks, with no complications and good wound healing documented. This pregnancy has been low risk with normal growth and a cephalic presentation at the most recent scan. She has no medical comorbidities. She has read about vaginal birth after caesarean online and asks you to explain the absolute risk of uterine rupture during a trial of labour. The hospital has on-site theatre and obstetric cover 24 hours a day.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 80 bpm, BMI 26 kg/m2
data_table.Examination: Symphysis fundal height 30 cm, cephalic presentation, well-healed Pfannenstiel scar
data_table.Investigations: Anomaly scan normal, glucose tolerance test normal, group B streptococcus screening pending

lead_in: Which figure most accurately reflects the absolute risk of symptomatic uterine rupture for her?

options.text: Approximately 5% in a planned vaginal birth after caesarean with one prior lower-segment scar
options.rationale: A 5% risk overstates uterine rupture by a factor of 10 and would inappropriately drive women away from VBAC. The actual figure is around 0.5%. (Source: RANZCOG - Birth after Previous Caesarean Section)
source_refs: RANZCOG - Birth after Previous Caesarean Section

options.text: Approximately 0.5% in a planned vaginal birth after caesarean with one prior lower-segment scar
options.rationale: The risk of symptomatic uterine rupture during trial of labour after one prior lower-segment caesarean is approximately 0.2 to 0.7% in Australian and international datasets. RANZCOG quotes 1 in 200 (approximately 0.5%) as the headline figure for counselling, with augmentation of labour roughly doubling this risk. (Source: RANZCOG - Birth after Previous Caesarean Section)
source_refs: RANZCOG - Birth after Previous Caesarean Section

options.text: Approximately 0.01% in a planned vaginal birth after caesarean with one prior lower-segment scar
options.rationale: 0.01% underestimates rupture risk by 50-fold and falsely reassures. The risk is real and requires informed consent with appropriate intrapartum monitoring. (Source: RANZCOG - Birth after Previous Caesarean Section)
source_refs: RANZCOG - Birth after Previous Caesarean Section

options.text: Approximately 10% in a planned vaginal birth after caesarean with one prior lower-segment scar
options.rationale: 10% is the rough rate for women with a prior classical (upper segment) caesarean undergoing trial of labour and is the basis for the contraindication. With a low-transverse scar the risk is approximately 0.5%. (Source: RANZCOG - Birth after Previous Caesarean Section)
source_refs: RANZCOG - Birth after Previous Caesarean Section

options.text: Approximately 2% in a planned vaginal birth after caesarean with one prior lower-segment scar
options.rationale: 2% overestimates rupture by approximately 4-fold and conflates symptomatic rupture with combined uterine-scar dehiscence rates seen on imaging. (Source: RANZCOG - Birth after Previous Caesarean Section)
source_refs: RANZCOG - Birth after Previous Caesarean Section

explanation.summary: Vaginal birth after one prior lower-segment caesarean is offered as a reasonable choice in Australia, with informed consent covering the success rate (around 60 to 80% achieve vaginal birth) and the rupture risk of approximately 0.5%. Risk modifiers include short inter-pregnancy interval under 18 months, no prior vaginal birth, induction of labour (particularly prostaglandins which roughly triple risk), and labour augmentation. VBAC should occur in a setting with 24-hour obstetric, anaesthetic, and neonatal cover and immediate theatre access.
key_points: Symptomatic uterine rupture with one prior LSCS: approximately 0.5% (1 in 200)
Prostaglandin induction roughly triples rupture risk; oxytocin augmentation roughly doubles it
Classical (vertical upper-segment) scar contraindicates trial of labour (approximately 10% rupture risk)
Continuous CTG monitoring is recommended during VBAC labour
explanation.pearls: Induction of VBAC labour with prostaglandins approximately triples the baseline rupture risk from around 0.5% to 1.5% - mechanical methods or oxytocin alone are preferred when induction is needed.

sources.label: RANZCOG - Birth after Previous Caesarean Section
RCOG Green-top Guideline 45 - Birth after Previous Caesarean

stem: You are the obstetric registrar called to the antenatal ward for a 28-year-old primigravida at 36+1 weeks. She has had 5 days of nausea, vomiting, anorexia, and right upper quadrant discomfort. Today she has become drowsy and developed mild jaundice. There is no headache, visual disturbance, or epigastric pain. Her blood pressure is mildly elevated but her reflexes are normal and there is no clonus. Urine dipstick shows 1+ protein and 2+ ketones. The fetal CTG is reassuring. She has no known liver disease. The consultant is en route. Her admission bloods have just returned.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 138/86 mmHg, HR 110 bpm, RR 24, Temp 37.4 degrees C
data_table.Examination: Mildly drowsy, jaundiced sclerae, RUQ tender, no asterixis, no focal deficit, fundal height 35 cm
data_table.Investigations: Bilirubin 78 micromol/L, ALT 320 U/L, AST 290 U/L, INR 1.9, glucose 2.4 mmol/L, ammonia 110 micromol/L, urate 0.58 mmol/L, platelets 168 x10^9/L, creatinine 124 micromol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence intravenous magnesium sulfate and antihypertensives for severe pre-eclampsia and continue expectant care
options.rationale: While there is overlap with pre-eclampsia, the dominant features (hypoglycaemia, marked coagulopathy without low platelets, hyperammonaemia, ketonuria) point to acute fatty liver of pregnancy which requires delivery, not magnesium prophylaxis alone. (Source: SOMANZ - Liver Disease in Pregnancy)

options.text: Arrange urgent delivery by caesarean section after correction of coagulopathy and hypoglycaemia
options.rationale: Acute fatty liver of pregnancy is suggested by hypoglycaemia, coagulopathy without HELLP-pattern thrombocytopaenia, hyperammonaemia, and modestly raised transaminases in the third trimester. Delivery is definitive and time-critical to halt progressive hepatic and renal failure. Coagulopathy and hypoglycaemia are corrected pre-operatively and tertiary care offers liver and renal support. (Source: SOMANZ - Liver Disease in Pregnancy)

options.text: Treat for viral hepatitis with supportive care while awaiting serology and avoid early delivery
options.rationale: Viral hepatitis typically presents with transaminases in the thousands rather than the hundreds and rarely produces the severe hypoglycaemia and coagulopathy pattern seen here. Awaiting serology delays delivery and worsens outcome in AFLP. (Source: SOMANZ - Liver Disease in Pregnancy)

options.text: Administer ursodeoxycholic acid orally and arrange outpatient hepatology review for intrahepatic cholestasis
options.rationale: Intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy presents with pruritus and elevated bile acids without hypoglycaemia, coagulopathy, or encephalopathy. The clinical picture here is incompatible with cholestasis. (Source: SOMANZ - Liver Disease in Pregnancy)

options.text: Arrange transjugular liver biopsy to confirm the diagnosis before considering delivery
options.rationale: Biopsy may show microvesicular steatosis but is not required for diagnosis or treatment. Coagulopathy makes biopsy hazardous and delivery is the priority. (Source: SOMANZ - Liver Disease in Pregnancy)

explanation.summary: Acute fatty liver of pregnancy presents in the third trimester with nausea, anorexia, abdominal pain, mild jaundice, and progressive multi-organ dysfunction. Hallmarks are hypoglycaemia, coagulopathy out of proportion to thrombocytopaenia, hyperammonaemia, hyperuricaemia, and modest transaminase rise (typically 300 to 500). Differentials are severe pre-eclampsia with HELLP and viral hepatitis; the Swansea criteria support the diagnosis. Delivery is the only definitive treatment. Maternal mortality is up to 18% and perinatal mortality 20 to 30% without timely delivery.
key_points: AFLP triad: hypoglycaemia, coagulopathy without HELLP-pattern thrombocytopaenia, hyperammonaemia
Transaminases typically 300 to 500 (lower than viral hepatitis)
Delivery is the only definitive treatment; coagulopathy and glucose corrected before operating
Swansea criteria support clinical diagnosis - liver biopsy is not required
explanation.pearls: Hypoglycaemia in a third-trimester woman with right upper quadrant pain and modestly elevated transaminases (300 to 500) suggests acute fatty liver of pregnancy until proven otherwise - delivery is the only definitive treatment.

sources.label: RANZCOG - Acute Fatty Liver of Pregnancy
BMJ Best Practice - Acute Fatty Liver of Pregnancy
